title: Can We Forget Our Duty While Demanding Our Rights?
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. The source pages confirm that the English newspaper quotation begins `Some of the biggest road projects`, not the OCR fragment `5016`. Other source-visible OCR errors, including `மாவட்ட நிலவியல்`, `மதுரவாயல்`, and words split or joined incorrectly at page breaks, are restored in the derived Tamil reading copy. The printed salutation is also restored. The canonical source files remain unchanged.
paragraphs: At a time when the AIADMK government complains that the Union government is neglecting Tamil Nadu's needs, should the Tamil Nadu government not at least come forward to spend properly the funds allocated to the state by the Union government? I.G. Reddy, Chief General Manager of the Chennai regional office of the National Highways Authority, has openly stated that irregularities are rampant in the implementation of Union highway projects in Tamil Nadu, and that national-highway projects worth ten thousand crore rupees have been brought to a standstill because the Tamil Nadu government is not cooperating.

A Union minister openly made the same accusation. He said he had requested an early appointment with the Tamil Nadu Chief Minister and would discuss all these projects with her. No report has appeared since then to suggest that such a discussion ever took place.

> The National Highways Authority undertakes various development works in every state, including the expansion and improvement of national highways. State governments must sign cooperation agreements for this purpose. The Tamil Nadu government refuses to sign cooperation agreements for new projects. It also refuses to honour earlier agreements already signed. Consequently, projects worth more than 10,000 crore rupees have come to a standstill in Tamil Nadu. > > The state government's non-cooperation continues in the form of delays in removing encroachments and handing over land, withholding permission for minerals including gravel and soil, withdrawing no-objection certificates issued by the Public Works Department, and delaying no-objection certificates. We have taken these problems to the Tamil Nadu government many times, but without result. In other states, the state governments themselves come forward, enter into agreements and implement highway projects. Here, there is not even a cooperation agreement. Such agreements are essential for implementing projects of this kind. To give one example, 2,614 hectares of land are required for national-highway expansion projects, but the Tamil Nadu government has still not issued the necessary order. > > The project to widen the Dindigul-Theni-Kumili road is still incomplete. This 135-kilometre project should have been completed by last August, but only eighty kilometres have been widened. This is the reality of the state government's cooperation with us on every project.
Mr Reddy has thus levelled a grave accusation against the Tamil Nadu government.

The state government has so far given no response to these charges. That itself makes the accusations appear true. The General Manager of the National Highways Authority was not expressing personal grievances. He says that because of the Tamil Nadu government's non-cooperation, projects worth more than 10,000 crore rupees that should have come to Tamil Nadu have not materialised.
Mr Reddy made several other allegations, which were listed in *Junior Vikatan*. Here are some of them:

> We give a highway contractor a list of the soil, gravel and other materials needed for the work, along with the work order. To purchase stone and gravel for building roads, contractors must obtain permission from the district Department of Geology and Mining. A combine of ruling-party members and officials compels them to pay a bribe of one hundred rupees per cubic metre to obtain this permission. They therefore have to pay enormous bribes. The same condition prevails in highway projects implemented by the state government. > > Although we complained to the Tamil Nadu government and the police about this corruption in the highways department, they did not register the complaints. Instead of accepting complaints from our contractors, the police register complaints lodged by an influential person against those contractors. We have written to the Chief Secretary of Tamil Nadu explaining the obstacles placed in the way of national-highway projects. > > Among the projects kept pending is the widening of the Chennai-Tirupati road. Work has been completed on the Andhra Pradesh side. In Tamil Nadu, however, work has not even begun because permission has not been granted. > > Because the state government does not cooperate, no contractor is willing to undertake National Highways Authority work in Tamil Nadu. Contractors even say they no longer want agreements they have already secured. > > The Chennai-Tirupati road and Chennai-Tada road, which were due for completion in October 2013; the Ennore-Manali Road Improvement Project, due in June; the Tiruchi-Karaikudi road project, due in May; the Krishnagiri-Walajapet road project, due in December; and the Chennai Port-Maduravoyal elevated-road project, due in September, have all been affected.

I myself have repeatedly spoken about the Chennai Port-Maduravoyal elevated-road project. But this regime has answered nothing. After waiting and waiting, the National Highways Authority finally went to the Madras High Court over the project.
In a petition filed last week in that case, Union government counsel Wilson stated:

> If the Tamil Nadu government's ban on the Maduravoyal elevated-road project continues, the Union government will immediately withdraw the project. Tamil Nadu will suffer a loss if the Union government abandons it. Eighty per cent of the cargo arriving at Chennai Port comes by road. > > Because Metro Rail work is under way, cargo vehicles now face difficulty reaching Chennai Port even during the hours in which they are permitted. Vehicles are allowed through the southern route only during specified night-time hours. About seventy per cent of the cargo arriving at the port comes from the south. If this road is built, the port will develop further. > > In reliance on this road, 121 acres of land have been acquired at Sriperumbudur for a container terminal costing 3,000 crore rupees. It has been decided to pay the landowners 235 crore rupees in compensation. There is also a plan to establish a dry port at Mappedu on the Chennai-Bengaluru road. The court must order the continuation of this road project. > > The National Highways Authority has constructed bridges and roads over seas, rivers and lakes in many places. Why, then, should a bridge and road not be built over the Cooum alone? Work worth 1,800 crore rupees was undertaken on this project, and about 900 crore rupees has already been spent. Pillars have been erected at several places. This road was conceived for the welfare of the public, not for the benefit of an individual. The court must therefore order its speedy implementation. It now takes one and a half hours to travel from Chennai Port to Maduravoyal. Once the project is completed, Maduravoyal can be reached from the port by elevated road in twenty-five minutes.
The High Court judges adjourned the case at the state government's request.

When the Union minister responsible for this department and its senior official have openly levelled so many accusations against the Tamil Nadu government, should the Chief Minister or the minister concerned not answer responsibly? Is it right to cite some paltry excuse and keep the Maduravoyal-Port elevated-road project, valued at 1,885 crore rupees, suspended for a year after about 900 crore rupees has already been spent? The company contracted to complete the work has filed a case seeking about 900 crore rupees as compensation for the delay. To whose account should that amount be charged? Does being the ruling party or Chief Minister mean one may do whatever one likes? Is it not the people's tax money that is being wasted?
Here is what *The Times of India* wrote on 23 November 2013:

> Some of the biggest road projects in the city have been hit by a severe staff shortage in the State Highways Department's metro wing, which is functioning at just 50% of the sanctioned strength. 63 projects, including grade separators at Anna Nagar, Moolakadai, Porur, and Thirumangalam, as well as rail over-bridges and foot over-bridges worth more than Rs. 1,325.8 crore, are being inordinately delayed because of this, say officials. There are only 14 assistant engineers against the 20 sanctioned, and they have their hands full. There are 152 new projects, including those for upgrading Inner Ring Road, EVR Salai and Anna Salai to “world standards”, for which “detailed project reports” need to be prepared. Several proposals seeking Japanese International Corporation Agency funding for projects worth Rs. 2,000 crore also need to be processed.
What is the government's answer to all this? Is it the DMK that has made this accusation?

Neither I, nor the Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam, nor the leader of Tamil Nadu's principal opposition party, nor the leaders of any other opposition party made the charges set out above. They were openly made by a responsible Union minister and a senior Union government official, and were published in the newspapers. It would therefore be proper for the Chief Minister to give immediate and adequate answers. When we accuse the Union government of failing to provide the financial assistance we requested, we must not give anyone reason to ask whether the state government has failed to spend properly the funds already allocated to it. Demanding the funds the state needs is our right! Spending allocated funds properly is our duty! Can we forget our duty while demanding our rights?
